title: Klein tunneling of the laser coherence
authors: Konstantin Manannikov, Sagie Gadasi, Ekaterina Mironova, Eran Bernstein, Andrei Poliakov, Nir Davidson, Alexander N. Poddubny
abstract: We study theoretically the lasing synchronization of the two arrays of lasers with the complex mode dispersion, separated by a spectrally detuned barrier. We demonstrate that for lasing at the Dirac point, the synchronization persists for an order of magnitude higher barriers than in the arrays with a usual parabolic dispersion or a purely dissipative coupling. We interpret this effect as the Klein tunneling of the laser coherence through the barrier. Our numerical findings are supported by an analysis of the delocalization of the linearized eigenmodes of the arrays, which enhances the synchronization.
summary: The study shows that laser arrays with linear (Dirac) dispersion can maintain frequency synchronization across much larger frequency barriers than those with standard parabolic dispersion. This enhanced robustness is attributed to the 'Klein tunneling' of laser coherence, where modes delocalize through the barrier.
main_problem: Investigating the robustness of frequency synchronization between two laser arrays separated by a spectrally detuned barrier, specifically exploring how different dispersion laws (linear/Dirac vs. parabolic) affect the synchronization threshold.
main_result: For linear (Dirac) dispersion, synchronization persists for barrier heights approximately an order of magnitude higher than for parabolic or flat dispersion, an effect interpreted as the Klein tunneling of laser coherence.
method: Numerical solution of nonlinear laser rate equations (Lugiato-Lefever-type) combined with linear analysis via diagonalization of a non-Hermitian effective Hamiltonian.
model_or_system: 1D and 2D (hexagonal/honeycomb) arrays of coupled lasers described by a non-Hermitian effective Hamiltonian including dispersive and dissipative coupling components.
key_observables: Probability of desynchronization (P_desync), nonlinear transmission of coherence (T), mode localization/delocalization, and complex eigenfrequencies (dispersion and loss).
important_parameters: Barrier height (delta_B), barrier width (N_B), pump rate (p/gamma), and dispersion type (linear/Dirac, parabolic, or flat).
main_assumptions: The nonlinear lasing solutions can be approximated by linear combinations of the lowest-loss eigenmodes of the effective Hamiltonian.
figures_summary: Fig 1 shows the 1D setup, coupling scheme, and band structure/transmission; Fig 2 compares desynchronization probability across different dispersion regimes; Supplemental figures show mode loss, localization, and the effect of pump strength.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the synchronization problem in laser arrays, presents the 1D model and the discovery of enhanced synchronization via Klein tunneling, extends the findings to 2D honeycomb lattices, and provides a detailed analysis of mode localization and symmetry effects in the supplement.
why_interesting: It demonstrates a direct analogy between a fundamental relativistic particle phenomenon (Klein tunneling) and the macroscopic synchronization of non-equilibrium, non-Hermitian classical oscillators, which is highly relevant to open quantum systems and quantum optics.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. (a) One-dimensional array of N = 100 coupled lasers with periodic boundary condition (PBC) and double barrier composed of NB = 3 lasers with frequency detun- ing ∆B. L and R are the left and right regions of an array, respectively, and B is the frequency detuned barrier that sep- arates them. (b) A complex coupling scheme is implemented to achieve a minimal-loss lasing solution at k points with ei- ther linear or parabolic dispersion. Solid (dashed) lines de- note dissipative (dispersive) coupling coefficients. (c-d) Lines: analytical calculation of band structure (solid) and loss land- scape (dashed) obtained from diagonalizing the effective non- Hermitian Hamiltonian Hnn′....

FIG. 2. (a) Probability of de-synchronization for barrier width NB = 10 and different ∆B for τ1 < 0 (linear disper- sion), τ1 > 0 (parabolic dispersion) and τ2 = 0 (flat disper- sion). (b) Same as (a), but for a reduced basis of only the Nm = 4 lowest-loss modes. (c-d) Color map represents the localization of the first 4 eigenmodes of the coupling matrix for different barrier heights and widths for (c) τ1 < 0 and (d) τ1 > 0. Lines on color plots mark the desynchroniza- tion threshold, obtained from the non-linear simulation, and agree well with the linear localization transition. The calcula- tions were performed for N = 100 coupled lasers with periodic boundary conditions, p = 1.5, and γ =...

FIG. 4. (a) Probability of desynchronization of the arrays left and right of the barrier for the trivial dispersion (blue dots) and for the Dirac dispersion (red dots). The dashed line indicates the position of the M point for the Dirac band structure. The inset shows the geometry of the laser array with a barrier in the middle, where green lasers are detuned by ∆B. (b) Dependence of critical barrier height for synchro- nization ∆∗ B on the nearest neighbor coupling value τNN. The blue line shows the frequency of the M point of the Brillouin zone. (c) Dependence of the normalized critical barrier height ˜∆∗ B(NB) = ∆∗ B(NB)/∆∗ B(1) on the width of the barrier, see Sec. S2 of Ref. [34] for...

FIG. 3. (a-b): Band structure of honeycomb laser array. Pic- ture (a) shows the frequencies of two eigenvalues of the Bloch Hamiltonian, while the loss of these eigenvalues is on (b). The minimal loss solution is localized around K and K′ points and has a linear dispersion relation. (c,d): Near and far field of the laser array with the linear dispersion, confirming lasing around the K and K′ points and a chiral phase structure.

Figure 3(a,b) shows the real (a) and imaginary parts (b) of ω(k) calculated by diagonalizing Eq. (6). The dispersion in Fig. 3(a) manifests characteristic Dirac cones, and the loss in Fig. 3(b) is minimized at the K points. Conversely, τNN = 0.1 and τNNN = 0.03i yield lasing at the Γ point of the Brillouin zone with parabolic (trivial) dispersion.
